title: Oil prices bring relief; monsoon, West Asia risks dim growth, inflation outlook
description: Though progress in US-Iran negotiations have helped moderate global crude oil prices, providing some breathing room, an erratic monsoon and lingering tensions in West Asia pose risks for growth and inflation, says the latest monthly economic review by India’s Ministry of Finance.“While easing oil prices and improving global supply-chain conditions may help alleviate some external pressures, uncertainties surrounding the monsoon and geopolitical developments in West Asia continue to pose downside risks to the growth and upside risks to the inflation outlook,” the report notes. It adds that even as the economy continues to exhibit resilience, emerging signs of moderation in industrial activity along with evolving inflationary pressures “demand closer attention ahead”.Monsoon emerges as key concernThe most pressing concern flagged in the review is the weather, due to the heavy influence of emerging El Niño conditions over the tropical Pacific Ocean. The southwest monsoon is so far expected to be well below the long-period average (LPA), with kharif sowing running 22.7 percent behind last year’s pace. While healthy reservoir levels and ample fertiliser stocks offer some cushion, the ministry noted that the spatial unevenness of rainfall, with nearly three-quarters of meteorological subdivisions reporting deficient or large-deficient rain, remains a genuine threat to the agricultural outlook. The report notes that as of June 28, cumulative rainfall remains 43 percent below the long-term average.Also Read: Gold and Silver Prices Add Fresh Pressure to India’s Inflation Basket“Delayed onset and weak progress of the southwest monsoon have weighed on kharif sowing, and the evolution and spatial distribution of monsoon rainfall will remain critical for the agricultural outlook,” the report says. However, with combined wheat and rice stocks exceeding 4.4 times the prescribed buffer norm, foodgrain stocks remain “comfortable” and provide a cushion against potential supply-side disruptions.Global oil markets cool, but risks persistThe report states that global energy markets cooled considerably through June, as progress in US-Iran negotiations helped pull Brent crude down from levels above $120 a barrel at the height of the crisis in April to under $80 a barrel, averaging around $89.4 a barrel by late June. Yet the ministry cautioned that this correction remains “fragile”, given continuing disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and the US Energy Information Administration’s recent upward revision of expected oil-production shut-ins in the region. “Global crude inventories are expected to remain under pressure in the near term, even as shipping and production conditions gradually normalise,” the report says.On the domestic front, the economy notched robust growth of 7.7 percent for 2025-26, with momentum carrying into the new fiscal year. Gross domestic product (GDP) growth in Q4 FY26 accelerated to 7.8 percent from 7 percent in Q4 FY25, powered by a sharp pickup in services (9.9 percent) and continued strength in industry (9.3 percent), alongside the strongest investment expansion recorded under the current GDP series.Also Read: India IIP Growth Slows to 4.1 Percent in March Amid West Asia WarHigh-frequency indicators such as e-way bill generation, PMI readings and electricity consumption suggest this momentum has largely held in FY27 so far, even as some indices, core industries output, fuel consumption, air travel and consumer confidence, point to a degree of cooling. “The recent de-escalation of geopolitical tensions in West Asia is expected to improve global risk sentiment, ease commodity price pressures, and support domestic economic activity in the months ahead,” the report notes.Fiscal position strengthensThe government’s finances also strengthened markedly. The Centre’s fiscal deficit narrowed to 4.4 percent of GDP in FY26, the lowest since 2018-19, aided by broad-based tax collections and a jump in non-tax revenue from higher Reserve Bank of India and public-sector dividends. Capital spending kept rising, up nearly 19 percent year-on-year in April, even as revenue expenditure held steady as a share of output.Current and capital flowsTrade performance was a particular bright spot, with combined merchandise and services exports growing nearly 16 percent year-on-year in May, and merchandise shipments hitting a record monthly high in May. The India-Oman trade pact and the upcoming India-UK agreement, along with expanded farm and marine market access to the European Union, are expected to sustain this momentum.Remittances, too, hit a record $155 billion for the year from $135.4 billion in FY25, defying fears that Gulf-related disruptions would dent inflows, a resilience the ministry attributes to remittances being driven more by overseas employment conditions than by financial-market sentiment. “This resilience is consistent with empirical findings that remittances are among the most stable components of external financing, remaining relatively insulated from episodes of financial market volatility and geopolitical uncertainty,” the report notes.Meanwhile, foreign investment told a more divided story. Foreign direct investment strengthened for a fourth straight month at $6.6 billion in April, but portfolio investors pulled out a combined $10.7 billion over April and May amid the regional risk-off mood, though early June data hint at a turnaround following the US-Iran agreement. The rupee, having weakened nearly 4 percent against the dollar since late February, has begun to stabilise as tensions ease.

title: Apple takes Epic fight over app store fees to the Supreme Court
description: Supreme Court will weigh if Apple contempt finding in Epic case is “erroneous.”
content:
Apple is hoping the Supreme Court will reverse a contempt finding that threatens to block the tech giant from charging high commission fees when developers divert iPhone users to non-Apple payment methods for app purchases.
The contempt finding came in a case where Epic Games accused Apple of violating a judicial order requiring changes to its App Store, which charged a 30 percent commission for using Apple payment methods and did not allow developer links to alternative payment methods.
That order required Apple to allow developers to include links to make payments outside the Apple ecosystem, but Apple did so only after requiring a 27 percent commission for allowing the link-outs. In December, Apple lost an appeal after defending its commission as reasonable. At that time, the 9th Circuit concluded that Apple violated the spirit of the order by charging fees so high that they “had a prohibitive effect” on developers who saw little benefit in updating apps to save only a small amount on fees.Read full article

title: New attack provides one more reason why AI browsers are a bad idea
description: Telling an LLM that 2 + 2 = 5 is enough to make it follow forbidden instructions.
content:
Makers of AI browsers make lofty promises. With a single prompt, users can ask one to find a restaurant in a particular part of town, reserve a table, invite a colleague to lunch, and email a confirmation. These makers are much more reticent about the risks of blurring the once fine line between browsing sites and asking a large language model a question or instructing it to take potentially sensitive actions.
LLM developers’ answer so far has been to build guardrails that make some requests off-limits. Developing software exploits, stealing credentials, or teaching how to build a pipe bomb are examples. The problem with this approach is that the guardrails are reactive and treat the symptoms rather than solve the root cause. It’s tantamount to the manufacturer of an unsafe vehicle advocating for new road designs rather than fixing the flaws that make it prone to accidents.
Lulling LLMs into an alternate reality
New research puts this predicament on sharp display. It demonstrates how a website can lull AI browsers into a false reality where the rules governing its behavior no longer apply. After that, an attacker has free rein to invoke all kinds of destructive actions, such as extracting code from a private repository or extracting credentials from the built-in password manager.Read full article
Comments

title: Google's new Nano Banana 2 Lite image model is its fastest and cheapest yet
description: They may not look as good, but Nano Banana 2 Lite images only take a few seconds to create.
content:
There are plenty of AI image-generation models these days, but the ones capable of quality outputs tend to be slow and expensive. Google DeepMind says its new image model, known as Nano Banana 2 Lite, offers the best balance of quality and speed. It's available today across the Google ecosystem, creating images in a fraction of the time it takes Google's beefier models.
The new model is part of the Gemini 3.1 family—it's technically called Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Image. On one hand, Google says this model is ideal for exploring ideas and "rapid-fire" prototyping, applications in which quality can take a backseat. However, the company has also provided some examples aimed at showing how close Nano Banana 2 Lite can get to the quality of its other image models.

      A comparison of Nano Banana 2 Lite with the non-Lite version.
        Credit:
          Google
      
In addition to the examples, Google also has Elo scores from Arena.ai ready to go, showing that users rate Nano Banana 2 Lite outputs almost as highly as the non-Lite versions. However, vibemarking doesn't always focus on the details that can make AI images look silly upon closer inspection. Google notes that Nano Banana 2 Lite tends to have more trouble with text, particularly if it's very small, and infographics are more likely to include incorrect data. Characters and people may also show poor consistency across iterations.Read full article

title: RFK Jr. stacks FDA panel with peptide peddlers as FDA scientists oppose access
description: Peptide drugs are popular, but FDA scientists warn they're untested, may be harmful.
content:
Anti-vaccine Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has stacked another federal advisory panel with allies, advancing his efforts to force the Food and Drug Administration to reverse its stance on a dozen popular but unproven injectable peptide drugs.
While FDA scientists determined in 2023 that the drugs lack evidence of benefits and may pose safety risks, Kennedy is a self-professed "big fan" of the dubious drugs and has vowed to "end" FDA's "aggressive suppression" of them.
On Monday, the Food and Drug Administration updated its list of advisors who will review the peptides, adding nine new members—almost all of whom personally promote peptide use and/or have financial ties to wellness clinics that offer peptide treatments. The additions bring the total number of advisors to 13, making it easy for the new members to determine the outcome of upcoming meetings.Read full article
Comments

title: The Uddhav Meltdown Has A Bengal Template — And A Two-Thirds Trap
description: The decline of Shiv Sena (UBT) reflects a broader trend among regional parties in India. Major losses in elections, such as UBT's dwindling legislative strength and TMC's internal coup, reveal a pattern where parties, often centered around a single leader, become vulnerable.
content: The erosion of Shiv Sena (UBT) is not a Maharashtra-specific event. It is the second time in two months that a major regional party built around one leader has been hollowed out through the same legal mechanism — and the Bengal case shows where it ends. This is starting to look like a pattern, not a one-off.The defection of UBT MLC Sachin Ahir on Tuesday (June 30) is best read as confirmation of a trajectory, not an isolated setback. Ahir, a close Uddhav Thackeray aide whose Worli base overlaps Aaditya Thackeray's own constituency, joined Eknath Shinde's Shiv Sena and filed for Deputy Chairman of the Legislative Council. The Shinde camp calls it the second phase of "Operation Tiger" — the first targeting MPs, the second now aimed at MLAs and MLCs.The sharper comparison is not the Sena's own 2022 split but what happened to the Trinamool Congress weeks earlier. The two cases share a mechanism, a sequence and an adversary — which is what makes "the Bengal template" more than a rhetorical device.Same Date, Same MethodThe clearest sign of a shared pattern is the timing. On June 22, a faction removed Mamata Banerjee as TMC chairperson — described by insiders as an internal coup — allegedly ending her near three-decade hold over the party she founded in 1998. The same day in Mumbai, six of the nine MPs elected on the UBT symbol merged into Shinde's faction, cutting Uddhav's Lok Sabha strength to three.The method is statutory, not incidental. The six MPs held a two-thirds share of the party's parliamentary group — the exact threshold the anti-defection law requires for a bloc to merge without disqualification. Bengal ran on the same arithmetic: 58 of the 80 TMC MLAs elected in 2026, past two-thirds, signed a letter installing the expelled Ritabrata Banerjee as Leader of the Opposition, a figure the rebels now put at 64. Separately, around 20 of the TMC's 28 MPs have signalled a move toward the NDA. In both cases the Tenth Schedule's merger clause — drafted to deter defection — became the instrument that legalised it.  The Structural Cause: Parties That Survive On A FaceThe mechanism explains how, not why both parties proved so easy to dismantle. Parties organised around a single charismatic leader, with thin institutional scaffolding and no firm ideological core, rise and fall with the personal fortunes of their supremo. Both the TMC and the UBT fit: each was a vehicle for a face, not a platform for a programme."Traditional parties, just like independents or independent parties, also have an age limit. As per my understanding, the average age of parties is around 30 to 40 years. Once a party reaches this threshold, there are pressures on the party for survival," said political analyst Amitabh Tiwari."National parties can survive because of their ideological positions and strong presence across the country… These regional parties in India are, anyway, controlled by families. The family-controlling people hold them together. But once a party loses the respect that the family commands, the ranks decline because the leaders then feel the head of the party is not able to look at their roots," he added.On the UBT setback, Tiwari said: "Most of the regional parties are in their second or third stage — a different dynasty, sort of. Second or third stage. What happens in that stage is that a lot of people are compared to the original regional leaders — for example, Balasaheb. A lot of people are likened to Balasaheb, but it's not the same as Balasaheb. Because of the lack of charisma and lack of popularity among the leaders, there is also a rebellion against these new leaders."Political commentator Rashid Kidwai located the fault deeper than the statute. "The two-thirds merger provision is not the root cause; it is a mechanism that political actors have learnt to use with increasing sophistication. The deeper issue is that many Indian regional parties have become personality-centric formations rather than institutional organisations. When political authority, electoral appeal, or access to power weakens, legislators begin to view parties less as ideological homes and more as political vehicles. The Shiv Sena and NCP splits demonstrated that the battle is no longer merely about ideology or loyalty, but about who controls the name, symbol, inheritance, and political future. In contemporary politics, parties increasingly risk becoming temporary containers rather than enduring institutions."  The fragility stays dormant in victory and turns fatal in defeat. Once the verdict turns, the leader's value to ambitious legislators collapses and the two-thirds exit becomes self-preservation, not ideological rupture. That engine turned over in Bengal first.The Defeats Came Before The DefectionsIn both states, the electoral collapse preceded — and triggered — the desertions. The TMC was reduced to roughly 80 seats in the Assembly election declared on May 4, 2026, ending 15 years of rule and down from 215 in 2021. Mamata herself lost Bhabanipur to Suvendu Adhikari, sworn in on May 9 as Bengal's first BJP chief minister. Tellingly, the man now running Bengal came not from the BJP's own stable but from Mamata's — Adhikari, once a Nandigram-era loyalist, had crossed over citing alienation.The UBT's decline was slower but followed the same logic. Cut to 20 MLAs in 2024, the decisive blow came locally: in January 2026 Uddhav's reunion with cousin Raj Thackeray, after two decades, failed to move voters, and the Mahayuti crossed the majority mark in the BMC — ending the family's near three-decade hold over India's wealthiest civic body. A brand that can no longer carry its home city is one whose carriers begin to leave.Where The Erosion Now PointsThe bleed is spreading from Parliament into the Assembly. Maharashtra minister Gulabrao Patil claims "Operation Tiger 2.0" is underway and that more than 14 of the UBT's 20 MLAs could cross over; a group of UBT MLAs reportedly met Shinde during the monsoon session. The number is not arbitrary — 14 is exactly two-thirds of 20.This is a claim by an adversary, not an established fact, and the analysis turns on that. What is established is the MP merger, the Ahir defection and the BMC loss; what is asserted is the MLA exodus. But the structural exposure is real: the party sits six legislators from the same statutory erasure in the assembly it has already suffered in the Lok Sabha.Fadnavis said, "Uddhav ji did not introspect, and this is the consequence." Uddhav countered  that this was "Operation Devendra" — a plot to weaken Fadnavis before he could emerge as a PM contender — an allegation Fadnavis rejected.Is this, then, a repeatable playbook against any regional party that loses power? Kidwai sees a wider shift. "The developments in Maharashtra and West Bengal suggest a broader pattern in Indian politics where electoral competition increasingly shifts away from the voter and towards managing the political field itself. Whether through engineered splits, defections, legal manoeuvres, candidate withdrawals, or the use of smaller political formations as vehicles for realignment, the methods have become more systematic and sophisticated. This is not unique to one state, but reflects a larger transformation in Indian politics where parties with stronger organisational machinery, resources, and institutional leverage enjoy a structural advantage. The concern is not simply who deploys these tactics, but that political competition itself is increasingly being shaped before voters cast their ballots."Where The Templates DivergeFirst, motive. In Bengal, the rebels still profess loyalty to Mamata Banerjee even as they sideline her nephew Abhishek — making that revolt partly a succession fight. The UBT crisis has no such ambiguity: it is a straight migration toward power, with Uddhav still atop a shrinking party and Aaditya his undisputed heir.Second, baseline. Mamata fell from an office she had held for years; Uddhav has held none since 2022. His response is limited to what little is left to him — touring the rebel MPs' constituencies and insisting he leads the "original and only Shiv Sena" — while his party brands the defectors not rebels but "traitors" who were bought off.The outcome will be settled less by mobilisation than by adjudication. Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla is expected to rule on the defections in both parties before the monsoon session opens in the third week of July. For the TMC, a parallel fight over name and symbol is headed to the Election Commission and the courts. Those rulings — not the constituency tours — will decide whether either supremo keeps anything beyond a label.(The article has expert views from political commentators Amitabh Tiwari and Rashid Kidwai)

title: WhatsApp rolls out username feature; not all users are happy
description: On June 29, WhatsApp introduced a new feature that enables users to reserve usernames for themselves, similar to what Telegram, another messaging app, launched in 2014. The WhatsApp feature will be rolled out over the coming months, but users can reserve usernames of their choice from this week; they will be notified once the feature becomes available in their country. The app will provide a username generator to help users—there are 3 billion of globally—make a pick. The feature will help creators, small businesses and organisations, who need to maintain a consistent presence online, claim their existing Instagram or Facebook username on WhatsApp.The Meta-owned messaging app said in its blog post on June 29 that this feature is a step towards increased privacy for users: “Usernames are our latest step to make WhatsApp even more private. There's no directory to browse and no suggestions—people will need to know your exact username to contact you for the first time. To help control who can reach you on WhatsApp with your username, we've built an optional username key that others will need to know to message you.”Users can choose to enable the feature if they want to keep their phone numbers private. At present, anyone who has your phone number can contact you on WhatsApp, but if you have enabled the new feature, they will need to know your username to contact you and will not be able to see your phone number. If you have already saved the person’s phone number, you will continue to see the name under which you saved that number.Your username will appear across direct messages, group chats and calls, and will be displayed with an @ symbol to distinguish it from a display name or phone number. To make sharing easier, WhatsApp will allow users to generate a QR code or a shareable link that directs others to their username. The feature is available on mobile devices and not on WhatsApp Web or Desktop.According to WhatsApp, the username should be between three and 35 characters long, can contain lowercase letters, digits, and special characters like underscores and periods. It cannot be a string of numbers only, it must be unique and can’t contain restricted words or phrases. Users can create, change or delete their username at any time, however, there will be a limit to the number of changes; previous usernames will become available to others after 14 days. Usernames can be transferred to new phone numbers.Also Read: WhatsApp Lawsuit Explained: Privacy Claims, Message Access and India’s DPDP ActWhatsApp will also offer an optional username key, which, when enabled, will require the other person to use both the username and the key to contact you. Existing contacts, people who have already chatted with, members of shared groups, those who have scanned your QR code, or anyone the user has messaged first will not need the key. The key is generated by WhatsApp and can be changed, regenerated or removed at any time. Enabling the feature will not affect existing conversations.The username feature has been announced days after Kunal Shah was announced as the new global head of WhatsApp, as the company aims to monetise its presence in India, where it has 500 million users. The broader vision is to transform WhatsApp into a ‘super app’ offering a wider range of services.“Timing is everything. Joined WhatsApp early enough to claim my username before we release this to the world. Time to get yours. A more private way to connect. Coming soon to your WhatsApp,” wrote Shah on X.In response to Shah’s post, Telegram wrote on X, “October 2014, is that you?”, while adding a link to its blog post from 12 years ago when it had rolled out the same feature.Everyone, however, is not as excited about the new feature. Entrepreneur and author Ankur Warikoo raised a red flag, saying scammers and fraudsters can create usernames that appear familiar and deceive users. “In a country such as India, this could be a disaster, if the right anti-abuse systems are not set up by WhatsApp,” he wrote on X.Jasveer Singh, CEO of KnotDating, a matrimony site, wrote on X, “WhatsApp just launched usernames. My first thought wasn't privacy - it was scams”, while Vijay Shekhar Sharma, founder and CEO of Paytm, wrote, “Soon you will have verified username on WhatsApp, and then unverified similar-sounding usernames… which in turn will…”WhatsApp has not mentioned if and how the username feature will be integrated with WhatsApp Payments.

title: Acti puts AI agents directly into your smartphone keyboard
description: Acti is betting the smartphone keyboard is the next home for AI assistants. The startup's new keyboard for iOS and Android works across apps and lets users create custom AI-powered shortcuts using natural language.

title: 2 കാര്യങ്ങൾ, സിയയുടെ സെർച്ച് ഹിസ്റ്ററി കണ്ട് ഞെട്ടി പൊലീസ്, ക്രൂരമായ കൊലപാതകം ആസൂത്രണം ചെയ്തത് ഇന്റർനെറ്റിലൂടെ; കേതൻ അഗർവാൾ വധക്കേസിൽ വഴിത്തിരിവ്
description: പുനെയിലെ വ്യവസായി കേതൻ അഗർവാളിന്റെ കൊലപാതകം പ്രതികൾ ഇന്റർനെറ്റിലൂടെ ആസൂത്രണം ചെയ്തതാണെന്ന് പൊലീസ് കണ്ടെത്തി. പ്രതി സിയയുടെ ഫോണിലെ സെർച്ച് ഹിസ്റ്ററിയിൽ പിടിക്കപ്പെടാതെ കൊലപാതകം നടത്താനുള്ള വഴികളാണ് തിരഞ്ഞതെന്ന് ഫൊറൻസിക് റിപ്പോർട്ടിൽ പറയുന്നു
content: <p>പുനെ: പുനെയിലെ യുവ വ്യവസായി കേതൻ അഗർവാളിനെ കൊലപ്പെടുത്താൻ പ്രതികൾ ആസൂത്രണം നടത്തിയത് ഇന്‍റർനെറ്റിലൂടെയെന്ന് പൊലീസ് കണ്ടത്തൽ. ഫൊറൻസിക് പരിശോധനയ്ക്കായി അയച്ച പ്രതികളുടെ മൊബൈൽ ഫോണുകളുടെ ഫൊറൻസിക് റിപ്പോർട്ടിലാണ് ഞെട്ടിക്കുന്ന വിവരങ്ങൾ. കൊലപാതകത്തിന് മുൻപ് സിയ നിയമ വിദഗ്ദരുമായി സംസാരിച്ചിരുന്നുവെന്നും പൊലീസ് കണ്ടത്തിയിട്ടുണ്ട്. പിടിക്കപ്പെടാതെ എങ്ങനെ കൊലപാതകം നടത്താം, മൊബൈൽ ലൊക്കേഷൻ ട്രാക്ക് ചെയ്യപ്പെടാതെ എങ്ങനെ സൂക്ഷിക്കാം തുടങ്ങിയ കാര്യങ്ങളാണ് പ്രതി സിയയുടെ ഫോണിലെ സേർച്ച് ഹിസ്റ്ററിയിൽ പ്രധാനമായും ഉണ്ടായിരുന്നത്. ചാറ്റ് ജി പി ടിയിലും, ഗൂഗിളിലും, യൂട്യൂബിലും എന്തിനേറെ ഡാർക്ക് വെബിലും സിയ കഴിഞ്ഞ ആറുമാസത്തിലേറെയായി തിരഞ്ഞിരുന്നത് ഈ കാര്യങ്ങൾ മാത്രം. ഒപ്പം സിയ നിയമ വിദഗ്ദരുമായി സംസാരിച്ചിരുന്നുവെന്നും അന്വേഷണ സംഘം കണ്ടത്തിയിട്ടുണ്ട്.</p><h2><strong>ഡിലീറ്റ് ചെയ്തെങ്കിലും രക്ഷയില്ല</strong></h2><p>കൊലപാതകത്തിനായി നടത്തിയ ഗൂഢാലോചനയുമായി ബന്ധപ്പെട്ട വിവരങ്ങൾ ആരും അറിയാതിരിക്കാൻ കഴിഞ്ഞ മൂന്ന് മാസക്കാലയളവിലുള്ള ചാറ്റുകളും വോയ്സ് നോട്ടുകളും സിയയും ചേതനും ഫോണുകളിൽനിന്ന് ഡിലീറ്റ് ചെയ്തിരുന്നു. ഇവ ഫൊറൻസിക് വിദഗ്‌ധരുടെ സഹായത്തോടെ വീണ്ടെടുത്തതോടെയാണ് കൃത്യമായി ആസൂത്രണം ചെയ്ത് സിയയും ചേതനും കൊലപാതകം നടത്തിയതെന്നതിന്‍റെ കൂടുതൽ തെളിവുകൾ പൊലീസിന് ലഭിച്ചത്. കൊലപാതകത്തിന് 34 മിനിറ്റ് മുമ്പ് സിയ ചേതനെ ഫോൺ ചെയ്‌തിരുന്നതായി അന്വേഷണ സംഘം കണ്ടെത്തിയിട്ടുണ്ട്. കൂടാതെ, പ്രതികളായ സിയയും ചേതനും കൃത്യത്തിന് ശേഷം രക്ഷപ്പെടാൻ ഉപയോഗിച്ച ആഡംബര കാറും, കേതൻ വിവാഹ നിശ്ചയ വേളയിൽ സിയയ്ക്ക് സമ്മാനിച്ച വിലകൂടിയ വജ്രാഭരണങ്ങളും പൊലീസ് പിടിച്ചെടുത്തിട്ടുണ്ട്. നിലവിൽ കൂടുതൽ ചോദ്യം ചെയ്യുന്നതിനായി ജൂലൈ 3 വരെ പ്രതികളെ പൊലീസ് കസ്റ്റഡിയിൽ വിട്ടിട്ടുണ്ട്.</p><p>അതേസമയം തിങ്കളാഴ്ച കോടതിയിൽ സിയയ്ക്ക് വേണ്ടി രണ്ട് അഭിഭാഷകർ ഹാജരായത് ആശയക്കുഴപ്പത്തിന് ഇടയാക്കി. അഭിഭാഷകരായ ആശുതോഷ് ശ്രീവാസ്തവയും, വിപുൽ ദുഷിംഗുമാണ് വക്കാലത്തുമായി ഹാജരായത്. സിയയെ പ്രതിനിധീകരിക്കുന്നതിനായി തന്നെ ഏൽപ്പിച്ചിരുന്നു എന്നായിരുന്നു ആശുതോഷ് ശ്രീവാസ്തവയുടെ കോടതിയിലെ അവകാശ വാദം. സിയ വക്കാലത്ത് ഒപ്പിട്ട് തന്നിട്ടാണ് താൻ കോടതിയിൽ എത്തിയതന്നും ഇയാൾ പറഞ്ഞു. എന്നാൽ അഭിഭാഷകൻ വിപുൽ ദുഷിംഗ് ആണ് തന്‍റെ വക്കീൽ എന്നും അല്ലാതെ ശ്രീവാസ്തവ അല്ലെന്ന് സിയ കോടതിയെ അറിയിക്കുകയായിരുന്നു. ദുഷിംഗിനെ ഔദ്യോഗികമായി നിയമിച്ചിട്ടുണ്ടെന്ന് സിയയുടെ കുടുംബവും ആവർത്തിച്ചു. പിന്നാലെ വഞ്ചനയിലൂടെ ചില നിയമപരമായ രേഖകളിൽ സിയയെ ഒപ്പിടിയിക്കാൻ ആശുതോഷ് ശ്രീവാസ്തവ ശ്രമിച്ചിരുന്നുവെന്ന് സഹോദരൻ സാഹിൽ മാധ്യമങ്ങളോട് പറഞ്ഞതിനെതിരെ ശ്രീവാസ്തവ രംഗത്ത് എത്തി. തനിക്കെതിരെ അപകീർത്തികരമായ ആരോപണങ്ങൾ നടത്തിയെന്ന് ആരോപിച്ച് സാഹിലിനെതിരെ ഇയാൾ മാനനഷ്ടത്തിന് കേസും ഫയൽ ചെയ്തിട്ടുണ്ട്.</p><p></p>

title: It started with one viral influencer complaining about Russia’s economy. Now a record 60% of Russians are pessimistic about their country’s outlook
description: Russia’s economy has been remarkably resilient, but it could be losing one of its most vital resources: public trust.
content:
Overnight, Victoria Bonya went from being a Russian influencer known for exercise routines and cosmetic endorsements into a catalyst for widespread public discontent about the state of her home country’s society and economy.



Bonya, a former television presenter in Russia who now makes a living as an influencer in Monaco, enjoyed the sort of virality most activists and politicians can only dream of in April, when she uploaded a screed critiquing Vladimir Putin’s handling of the Russian economy. The 18-minute video, posted to Instagram, has received around 32 million views and 1.7 million likes.



Bonya’s laundry list of grievances attacked everything from the government’s failed response to extreme flooding in southern Russia earlier this year, to rising inflation and taxes hurting households. The widely viewed and reshared video included a direct message to Putin: “You know what the risk is?” Bonya asked in the video. “That people will stop being afraid, ​and they’re being squeezed into a coiled spring, and that one day that coiled spring will shoot out.”



While Russians have yet to air their disapproval of the status quo in a Bolshevik-style rebellion (which some Kremlin leaders cautioned to be a real possibility in the turbulent weeks after Bonya’s video went viral), the country’s economy is starting to show strain at the most basic level: its people. 



Russians might be famous for their pessimism. Long a middling country in world happiness rankings, cross-cultural studies have shown the archetype of the brooding and gloomy Russian as described by Dostoevsky and Tolstoy might actually be a learned concept. But years into a war that has left Russians excluded from the world and saddled with broken promises from the government, official readings of economic pessimism have recently hit record highs, threatening the Kremlin’s tenuous hold on its society. 



Losing the people



Putin might scoff at the idea. Four years on from the invasion of Ukraine, Russia has proved remarkably resilient, shouldering sanctions, rocketing inflation, and depleted fiscal reserves. But Putin’s dogged wartime economy is quickly running out of a resource that has proven vital to shoring up social stability: confidence in their leader that one day, the sacrifices of average Russians will be worth it.



Russia has recorded a historic rise in economic pessimism this year, according to a Gallup poll published Tuesday. A record 60% of Russians said economic conditions were getting worse, up from 39% last year and the first time in the two decades Gallup has conducted the poll that pessimism represented the majority view. Only 27% of Russians said economic conditions were improving, and 56% considered their living standards to be worsening, also a record high.



Over the course of the war, Russians have been in a sort of social truce with the Kremlin. A concept analysts have termed “everyday patriotism,” the deal allowed Russians to keep living their prewar lives more or less uninterrupted, as long as they refrained from dissenting against the state or criticizing the war effort. 



Russia has been able to weather Western sanctions largely by ramping up trade with nonaligned countries, particularly India and China. Meanwhile, explosive growth in the military economy and stimulus spending on infrastructure and social benefits helped Russian households stay afloat, despite stoking inflation. 



Broken promises



But that compact is fracturing. Forced to cover for its military expenses, the Russian government announced hikes to its value-added tax last year, rising from 20% to 22%, a move some in Russia saw as a reneging on Putin’s pledge not to raise taxes before 2030. The new taxes have weighed on employment and forced small businesses to shut down. 



Those mechanisms are showing signs of severe strain. Russia’s liquid sovereign wealth fund assets are now worth 1.8% of GDP, compared to 6.5% at the start of the war, according to a report published earlier this month by the Kiel Institute, a think tank in Germany. The Kremlin’s fiscal picture has grown increasingly dire too, as its target budget deficit for the year was quickly surpassed within the first quarter. 



Oil and gas revenues, one of the only lifelines propping up Russian spending during the war, also collapsed 45% in the first three months of 2026 compared to a year prior, according to the report. Before the war in the Middle East, a global oversupply weighed on demand for Russian crude, while Ukrainian military strikes have been successfully limiting output at Russian refineries for months. 



High oil prices due to the standoff in the Strait of Hormuz granted Putin a temporary reprieve, but a labor shortage and constrained manufacturing capacity have complicated the Kremlin’s hopes of funneling more money into its defense sector.



“The fundamental constraint facing Russia today is not access to money but access to people, technology, and productive capacity,” Matthew Klein, an economist and coauthor of the Kiel Institute’s analysis, wrote in the report.



“The government can mobilize additional financial resources, but with labor shortages at record levels and sanctions restricting access to critical imports, higher spending increasingly risks generating inflation rather than greater military output,” he added.



For ordinary Russians, the breakdown is reflected in the declining economic mood. The Gallup poll found a rising share of Russians are losing faith in the country’s institutions, including the military and the national government. When asked about their employment prospects, 58% of respondents said it was a bad time to find a job, the highest rate since 2021, when Russian employment was still recovering from the pandemic.



Russia’s economy has been in mostly good health since the war began—sluggish, but never falling prey to the crash many analysts predicted. But faced with glacial progress on front lines in Ukraine, and with Russia’s defenses now dealing with drone attacks striking deep into its territory, Putin’s commitments to keep his war machine running are likely going nowhere. It’s getting harder to finance that effort while also keeping the public on his side.
This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

title: Innovative companies in India
description: India's dynamic business landscape is being transformed by a new generation of visionary enterprises that are redefining industry standards through innovation, strategic thinking, and purposeful growth. These forward-looking organizations are driving change across sectors by embracing cutting-edge technologies, sustainable practices, customer-centric approaches, and disruptive business models. Their commitment to excellence, adaptability, and long-term impact is not only strengthening India's economic ecosystem but also enhancing its global competitiveness. This curated recognition celebrates companies that continue to push boundaries, inspire progress, and contribute significantly to shaping the future of business and innovation in India.Sungrow India Pvt. Ltd.Sunil Badesra, Country DirectorSungrow, a global leader in renewable energy technology, has pioneered sustainable power solutions for over 29 years. As of Dec 2025, Sungrow has installed over 1000 GW of power electronic converters worldwide. The company is recognized as the world's most bankable PV inverter and energy storage company (BloombergNEF). Its innovations power clean energy projects across the globe, supported by a network of 520 service outlets guaranteeing excellent customer experiences. At Sungrow, we're committed to bridging to a sustainable future through cutting-edge technology and unparalleled service. Since its establishment in 1997, the Company has been concentrating on the field of new energy power generation, adhering to market demand orientation, and taking technological innovation as the propellant for development. The company has cultivated a professional R&D team with solid R&D experiences and strong capabilities for independent innovation.LocatR.ai Hemant Agarwal, FounderBuilding the Intelligence Layer for Modern LogisticsLocatR.ai is an AI-powered logistics intelligence platform transforming how businesses manage fleets, field operations, and last-mile delivery. Founded by Hemant Agarwal, the company combines transport management, workforce tracking, computer vision, IoT, and real-time analytics into a unified operating system that delivers complete visibility across assets, orders, and personnel.Built for India’s operating realities, LocatR.ai performs reliably even in low-bandwidth environments, enabling seamless execution across complex logistics networks. Its defining innovation is “ground-truth intelligence” - validating operational events through verifiable, data-backed evidence rather than simply tracking movement.Trusted by enterprises including Exide Industries, Meesho, Flipkart, ElasticRun, and LoadShare, the platform has helped reduce return-related losses by over 80% while cutting dispute resolution times from months to less than an hour. By turning operational data into actionable intelligence, LocatR.ai is helping build a more transparent, accountable, and efficient logistics ecosystem.MiBOT Ventures India Pvt. Ltd.Vivek Dilip, CEOMiBOT is building a hardware-agnostic embodied AI platform for real-time safety, surveillance, inspection, and compliance intelligence across high-risk enterprise environments like Construction, Oil & Gas, Power, Manufacturing, Airports & Ports.MiBOT's platform integrates seamlessly with existing CCTV infrastructure and Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs). MiBOT is the first company in the world to  to detect 100+ safety, operational, compliance, and surveillance anomalies in real time.Bizwin Solutions Pvt. Ltd.Aurijit Ganguli, Managing DirectorMost B2B companies have strong offerings and credible delivery. What holds them back is the sales system built around them.Bizwin transforms that system to deliver predictable revenue for its B2B clients across IT, Manufacturing, and Professional Services industries.What make Bizwin genuinely innovative are its proprietary SAGE framework and ADVICE methodology.  Leveraging these, Bizwin embeds senior practitioners — averaging 30+ years global sales experience— directly inside client sales teams. The result is a structured, self-sustaining revenue engine, not a consultant's report.The outcomes are real and measurable: 30–60% revenue growth within 12 months, proposal win rates improvement from 20% to 40% in 5 months, and many more.Equally interesting is Bizwin’s commercial model. It charges 1% success fee on incremental revenue generated – one of the few consulting companies in the world that stakes its fee on the client's growth.BuildingWorld.aiMukesh Dhirajlal & Daksha Mukesh, DirectorsIndia’s construction and interiors sector is worth more than ₹3 lakh crore annually, yet homeowners still struggle to bridge the gap between inspiration and execution. BuildingWorld.ai was created to solve that problem.Founded in 2022, the AI-powered visual commerce platform enables users to visualize spaces, identify materials from images, discover design ideas, source products, and connect with verified suppliers and professionals—all within a single ecosystem. Powered by six proprietary in-house AI tools trained on Indian design data, the platform is building digital infrastructure for the country’s-built environment.Ahead of launch, BuildingWorld.ai has aggregated 77,500+ products, 19,000+ curated design inspirations, 4,200+ brands, 83,600+ dealers, and 6,600+ professionals, creating one of India’s largest design-to-procurement networks.The company is jointly led by architect Mukesh Dhirajlal, Daksha Mukesh and Nikita Gandhi, combining decades of industry expertise with a shared vision to transform how India designs, sources, and builds. BurgrillShreh Madan, Co-founder & CMO Founded in 2016, Burgrill has grown into one of India’s leading homegrown QSR burger brands with 70+ outlets across the country. As the OGs of grilled burgers in India, we set out to prove that fast food can be both delicious and better for you.Long before protein became a mainstream conversation, Burgrill was building its menu around high-protein, wholesome ingredients. From grilled burgers and spinach tortilla wraps to an entire range of healthy bowls, we’ve consistently offered better alternatives without compromising on taste.Positioned in the mass-premium segment, Burgrill combines quality, affordability, and innovation to cater to the evolving Indian consumer palette. With a grilling-first vision, centralized quality control, and a commitment to mindful eating, Burgrill continues to redefine what modern QSR dining can look like in India.iOTA Infotech Pvt. Ltd.Rahul Layal,Founder & CEOCreativity was an afterthought in North India’s creative landscape, until the emergence of iOTA - A Creative Agency founded by Rahul Layal with the ethos that world-class work did not need a metropolitan address, it can be built from anywhere, even a Tier-2 City. iOTA has since evolved into a global creative company with 150+ members across the world, delivering genre-defining work across India, North America, Dubai and the Gulf region. The company invests heavily in R&D and emerging technology, weaving AI into its creative and growth workflows to stay ahead of where brands are headed next. The agency has worked with legacy brands and industry juggernauts like Reckitt, Tata 1mg, Konica Minolta, Pepsi, Wendy’s, XYXX, Milton, CK12, Muviq and YouTube to name a few. iOTA boasts a decorated portfolio of 40+ awards & titles, courtesy of its great work and its inclusive & progressive work-culture, which is reflected through their remarkable client & team retention and their widely beloved social media presence.AASOKAMonica Malhotra Kandhari, MDAASOKA is a comprehensive blended learning solution that provides a customised curriculum solution for K-12. AASOKA works with schools to provide them with the tools and services needed to upgrade both administrative and teaching-learning processes. The NEP 2020 and NCF-aligned model curriculum ensures interactive, engaging learning experiences for the 21st-century learners. Catering to CBSE, ICSE, and various state boards, AASOKA has partnered with 5,500+ schools, benefiting over 10,00,000 students and training 80,000+ teachers annually.AASOKA also offers AASOKA WizKids and AI STEAM Lab for students aged 4+ and 8+, respectively. WizKids builds foundational literacy, numeracy, and computational thinking through interactive activities and a screenless coding bot, while AI STEAM Lab promotes hands-on learning, creativity, and problem-solving across Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics (STEAM).AASOKA was ranked no.1 in the World’s Top EdTech Rising Stars of 2025 by TIME and Statista.Mad Result  Abhishek Karnik, Founder  In 2019, when Abhishek Karnik started Mad Result from a small Mumbai setup, it was based on one simple belief of how marketing should be defined in terms of revenue rather than vanity matrix. That belief, seven years ago, has since turned into one of the fastest-growing performance marketing agencies in India.The numbers reveal the narrative of growth. Mad Result scaled from its initial clients to 200+ brands across real estate, BFSI, and e-commerce, managing ₹34 crore+ in ad spends, delivering 200 million+ impressions over 3,000+ campaigns with over ₹150 crore collective client revenue. Its journey from owning a steady 6x ROI to being acclaimed as one of the best performance marketing companies in Mumbai has been legendary."We grew by obsessing over outcomes clients could bank, not just clicks," says Abhishek Karnik, Founder of Mad Result.The next chapter has already begun.Capstone People ConsultingDr Sujaya Banerjee, CEODr Sujaya Banerjee, CEO Capstone People Consulting (Mumbai, Singapore) - is a much-admired Global Expert Educator in the Leadership and Culture space. She is associated with influencing leading organizations across the Globe through Duke Corporate Education and is a post Merger and Acquisition Integration Expert - Redefining Leadership, Culture Transformation and the Future of Work organizations across the world.In an era shaped by disruption, change and evolving workforce aspirations, Dr Sujaya stands out as one of Asia’s most influential voices in Leadership and Culture Transformation and is also a prominent Woman Leadership Thought Leader.Dr Banerjee has built a purpose-driven Consulting powerhouse that partners with 300 plus  leading organizations globally, regularly manifests reviews of 5/5 and has an NPS of 100. Under her leadership, Capstone is recognised for its Innovative solutions in the areas of Workplace Culture, Leadership Capability-Building, Executive Coaching, DEI and High-Performance transformation. Recognized as Forbes Most Valued Companies for 2025, Siliconindia’s Best OD & Leadership Development 2025, Top 10 Women to Follow in 2026, Dr Sujaya continues to redefine modern leadership, proving that organizations shaping the future are those that put People at the heart of transformation.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: Trump's plan to redesign every .gov website leads to AI-designed horrors
description: A year in, National Design Studio delays plan to update government web standards.
content:
President Donald Trump’s plan to “fill the digital potholes” and use AI to quickly redesign every government website isn’t going very well.
Last August, Trump created the National Design Studio, or NDS, by executive order. A temporary DOGE-like entity that answers only to the president, NDS was tasked with creating new standards to update the US Web Design System (USWDS) and overhaul 27,000 dot-gov websites in just three years. At the end of this so-called “America by Design” initiative, the government’s “design language” would supposedly be more usable and beautiful, Trump expected.
However, that monumental task—assigned to a small team under a short timeframe—was seemingly made harder by DOGE's deep cuts to agencies previously responsible for improving government websites, including dismantling the 18F technology unit and restructuring the US Digital Service into DOGE.Read full article
Comments

title: SwaLay Digital: Where music distribution meets ownership
description: An Indian company has created a complete rights protection system, including audio fingerprinting, content security, and government copyright registration, within a single music distribution platform. No Indian or global distributor has tried this on a large scale.SwaLay Digital, an Indian music distribution company operated by TalantonCore Group, is positioning itself as one of the world's most complete rights protection platforms for independent artists. It combines music distribution with its own content security technology and India's first built-in copyright registration tool. The company distributes music to over 50 global and Indian streaming platforms, including Spotify, Apple Music, JioSaavn, and Amazon Music. For the last two years, it has focused on building a rights framework entirely in-house. This system goes far beyond what any Indian distributor, and most global ones, currently provide.At the heart of this infrastructure is My D.N.A™️ (Digital Notarization & Authentication), a tool launched this year. It allows artists to file an official Indian copyright application without leaving the SwaLay platform. The process results in a copyright registration that is recognized under India's Copyright Act and the Information Technology Act. Artists can check the status of their application in real time from their existing portal."Copyright protection and distribution are the easy parts now," said Nikhil Jain, founder of SwaLay Digital. "The real problem nobody was solving is what happens after the music is out, who monitors for theft, who protects the rights, and who helps the artist truly own what they created. We built the company around that question."In addition to copyright registration, SwaLay has integrated ACRCloud and Shazam's audio fingerprinting service into its platform. This enables real-time detection of unauthorized use of an artist's music online, even if the music has been pitch-shifted, sped up, or partially reproduced. Audio files on the platform are available only through time-limited presigned URLs, which block direct downloads or redistributions by third parties. Each release automatically creates a verified SmartLink page, serving as a permanent public record of ownership.The combination of these tools, all developed and maintained in-house instead of relying on third-party services, places SwaLay in a small group of platforms worldwide that view rights protection as an essential aspect of distribution, not just an optional feature.This launch comes at a crucial time for India's independent music market. The country produced over 130,000 new releases in 2024, within an industry valued at around ₹2,100 crore. Most of those artists lack formal copyright protection for their work. Historically, the government registration process has required physical paperwork, legal expertise, and weeks of processing time, which are often beyond the reach of working musicians."An artist should be able to distribute their music, market it, and register the copyright all in one click from home," Jain stated. "That workflow didn't exist in India before we created it." SwaLay plans to expand its public rollout later in 2026, with international growth of its system aimed at Southeast Asia and the South Asian diaspora market.For more information, please visit: https://swalay.com/The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: The US going 100% EV by 2040 would save more than 100k lives, study says
description: Much of it comes from heavy-duty trucks and buses that burn diesel.
content:
Although climate change is the primary motivation behind electric vehicle adoption, it isn't the only consideration. Removing internal combustion engines from the road directly saves lives by reducing airborne pollutants that can cause and trigger asthma and other lung diseases.
Now, a report from the International Council on Clean Transportation has tried to quantify that effect, comparing various electrification scenarios over the next couple of decades. Currently, more than 41,800 premature deaths are attributable to air pollution from road transport, the ICCT says.
We've long known that living near a busy road is associated with worse health outcomes. Combustion products like nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), particulates (PMs), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are all found around highways and busy intersections in concentrations high enough to cause health effects, and studies have repeatedly shown that living close to a major roadway is associated with increased mortality.Read full article
Comments

title: Used Renault Clio Renaultsport 2013-2016 review
description:
New Renaultsport hot hatch has sophistication, but can it excite when direct rivals like the Ford Fiesta ST have perfected the formula?

Few cars have reigned as class leader for as long as the Renault Clio Renaultsport.Every previous iteration of it has been our hot supermini of choice for a simple reason: each was an enthusiast’s dream.Fast, light, cheap and adjustable, with a heady 2.0-litre naturally aspirated engine and twangy Cup chassis, past versions positively clamoured to be driven hard and devoured track duties just as readily as they spiced up a commute.To anyone lucky enough to have spent a decade and a half behind its oversized steering wheel, the Renaultsport Renault Clio was a formula of devilish perfection. But apparently not one immune to change.With an all-new Renault Clio comes a seismic change to Renaultsport’s established approach. Like the standard model, the RS 200 must now be bought with five doors. Its engine has been downsized and turbocharged. And the gearbox has been automated. Although a more track-focussed RS 220 Trophy has recently been added.These changes are intended to make the former tearaway a more appealing prospect to a broader cross-section of buyers.But can a softer, more sensible Clio RS live up to our lofty expectations?

title: ‘You can expect prices to be high and stay high’: Domestic airfare is skyrocketing faster than international flight costs, despite using less jet fuel
description: Jet fuel costs doubled over the course of the Iran war, but other factors are behind the rising domestic travel costs.
content:
A summer travel paradox has emerged: Even though international flights use considerably more jet fuel than domestic travel, the cost to fly within the U.S. has skyrocketed at rates far beyond that of flying abroad.



According to data from airfare search engine Skiplagged, domestic flight price growth has increased 23.2% from March 2025 to this month, while international flight costs have increased 11.5% in the same time period. This summer marks the highest domestic passenger prices for the season since 2022.



Compared to international flights, which consume 15,000 to 30,000 gallons of fuel—about 1,500 to 3,000 per hour as a result of larger aircraft and long-haul routes—domestic flights use about 1,800 to 2,7000 gallons per trip, or about 750 to 900 gallons per hour.



Jet fuel costs nearly doubled during the Iran war, with supplies dwindling in some parts of the world as a result of halted traffic at the Strait of Hormuz, the choke point through which 20% of global oil usually flows. Rising fuel costs caused panic in the airline industry: Willie Walsh, the outgoing director general of the International Air Transport Association (IATA), warned this month the global airline industry’s profits would be cut in half as a result of tepid demand, culminating in the worst financial year for aviation since the pandemic.



The anxieties, however, did not meaningfully materialize. Instead, the rising airfare costs indicate high demand for summer travel, combined with airlines successfully making a series of moves that effectively protected them from supply-chain uncertainty from the war, such as leaning into premiumization and slashing certain routes. 



“It’s kind of a glass half full kind of scenario, where I think the outcome was not as bad as many predicted,” Christopher Anderson, a Cornell University professor of services management who studies the airline industry, told Fortune. “Because it wasn’t as bad, and the capacity is not there…we’re seeing elevated prices.”



The resilience is good news for the airline industry—but the pattern of increased prices ahead of a busy summer travel season also exposes the large portion of the American population that is now facing high travel costs, even to fly within the country. It’s yet another example of the K-shaped economy in action, where the wealthy can splurge on expensive airfare while most other households weigh tough travel decisions.



“Here in the U.S., we have a very bifurcated economy,” Anderson said. “And a lot of this turmoil that we’re seeing is the effects more on one segment of that economy than the other.”



What’s behind the domestic travel cost paradox?



Unlike increased road travel costs, which Americans experience in real time when they refuel their cars, air travel is only partially determined by the cost of jet fuel. Airline customers may be paying higher prices because of elevated fuel costs now, despite their travel being weeks or months in the future. Moreover, airlines are constantly managing routes and prices to respond to competitors. Part of this is a result of the Iran war. American Airlines announced this month it would suspend six “select routes” within the U.S. during August and September amid higher fuel costs, but part of it is the cost of doing business.



“I would absolutely say we’ve seen a dramatic increase in fuel prices, but without a doubt the entirety of that fuel cost has not been passed on to consumers,” Anderson said. “There’s obviously a correlation between airfares and fuel prices, but it’s not as strong as one might think.”



While some fare increases can be explained with higher costs, Anderson argued much of it can be explained by decisions airlines like American have made to cut capacity and focus on profits. However, resilient summer travel has meant higher demand for fewer flights, enabling airlines to keep prices higher. American Airlines expects similar profits this year as in 2025, when fuel prices were much lower.



“People still want to travel, and travel is still a bargain,” American CEO Robert Isom told Bloomberg TV last month.



After Spirit Airlines ended operations earlier this year, carriers saw an influx in the budget airline’s customers, further incentivizing them to keep fares higher, but airlines aren’t just getting more customers from budget airlines. The premiumization of the flying experience encourages travelers to upgrade their experiences. And not only are premium seats more expensive than regular economy tickets, but they tend to increase in price more than their more affordable alternatives.



“[Airlines] have a lot more pricing power, and so they definitely do recover those higher fuel prices in some of the more non-entry-level cabin assortments,” Anderson said.



Conversely, international travel costs have increased more modestly over this time because there hasn’t been the same changes in demand. There are often fewer international flights to begin with, meaning airlines don’t need to make as many changes to capacity in order to have full flights.



What can consumers expect of air travel costs?



Even with the Strait of Hormuz reopening and oil prices falling, airfare is expected to remain sky-high. Jet fuel is hovering around $2.87 per gallon, down from an April high of nearly $5, but according to Deutsche Bank Securities, airlines raised prices as recently as two weeks ago.



Part of the persistently high airfare is due to sustained demand, but part of it is also that it takes airlines time to add capacity back, Anderson said. Still, he doesn’t expect ticket costs to be lower in a few months for Americans who delayed making travel plans, banking on cheaper prices. There’s a chance budget airlines like Frontier expand their own capacity in the wake of Spirit Airlines’ exit—but more likely, airfare will move in the same direction as the aircraft: up.



“As someone looking for air travel, you can expect prices to be high and stay high for the near term,” Anderson said. “It’s not like air travel is going to get insanely inexpensive anytime soon.”
This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

title: IVAC marks major global wellness milestone with guests from 65+ countries
description: Indus Valley Ayurvedic Centre (IVAC), one of India’s most recognised Ayurvedic healing institutions, today announced a significant operational milestone: more than 50,000 guests have now completed therapeutic programmes at its Mysuru campus, with visitors recorded from over 65 countries across six continents. The centre also reports a guest satisfaction rating of 4.8 out of 5, based on independently verified reviews.The milestone positions IVAC among a small group of Ayurvedic institutions worldwide to have delivered physician-supervised classical treatments at this scale, and coincides with a broader shift in global wellness travel, from short-stay relaxation to structured, evidence-informed healing programmes.Milestone at a Glance50,000+ guests have completed healing programmes since the centre’s foundingGuests from 65+ countries, across Europe, the Americas, Asia-Pacific, the Middle East, and Africa8,000+ guests who have returned for repeat programmes, a metric the centre considers its core measure of outcome quality4.8 / 5 average satisfaction rating across independently submitted guest reviews25+ years of continuous physician-supervised Ayurvedic practice under a single founding medical director“This milestone is not simply a number. It is 50,000 individual decisions by people around the world to invest in their health through classical Ayurveda. Each guest arrived with a specific condition, a specific goal, or a specific need for restoration. That trust is what drives us to maintain the clinical rigour and personalisation that Ayurveda demands.”- Dr. Talavane Krishna, Founder and Medical Director, IVACWhy the Milestone Matters: A Shifting Global MarketThe timing of the announcement reflects a structural change in the global wellness industry. The Global Wellness Institute estimates the wellness economy at over USD 5.6 trillion, with wellness tourism emerging as one of its fastest-growing segments. Travellers are increasingly choosing destinations based on the clinical credibility and long-term health outcomes they offer, not merely the luxury of the experience.India, as the country of Ayurveda’s origin, holds a structural advantage in this environment. However, industry observers note that the advantage is only realised by institutions that deliver Ayurveda in its classical, physician-supervised form, a distinction that separates outcomes-focused centres from the broader ‘wellness retreat’ category.IVAC’s guest data, particularly the high proportion of guests from outside India and the volume of repeat visitors, is cited by the centre as evidence that physician-led classical Ayurveda commands an audience that purely spa-oriented offerings cannot easily replicate.About IVAC’s Clinical ModelFounded by Dr. Talavane Krishna, a qualified allopathic physician and anaesthesiologist who retrained in classical Ayurveda, IVAC operates on the principle that Ayurveda is most effective when delivered as a structured healing system rather than an experiential product. The centre’s approach begins with a detailed individual assessment, evaluating prakriti (constitutional type), current imbalances, and health history, before any therapy is prescribed.Panchakarma, the centre’s signature offering, is administered under the direct supervision of qualified Ayurvedic physicians and follows classical preparatory, treatment, and recovery protocols. The centre’s therapist training programme is conducted in-house to maintain consistency of technique and depth of knowledge across the guest experience.Located in Mysuru, Karnataka, a city with deep historical associations with yoga, Ayurveda, and traditional learning, IVAC benefits from a cultural environment in which Ayurvedic practice has existed continuously for centuries. The centre treats this context as a material part of the healing environment it offers guests.“Reaching 50,000 guests from over 65 countries is a meaningful data point for us, but the number we watch most closely is returning guests. More than 8,000 people have come back. In a category where the quality of outcomes is everything, repeat visits are the most honest signal we have.”-IVAC Guest Relations, Indus Valley Ayurvedic CentreWhat This Milestone Signals for Wellness Tourism in IndiaIVAC’s achievement is indicative of a broader opportunity for Karnataka and India. Mysuru, positioned as a hub for traditional knowledge systems and increasingly recognised on international wellness itineraries, is one of a small number of Indian cities with the cultural infrastructure, trained practitioner base, and institutional legacy to anchor a credible global wellness tourism brand.As interest in preventive health Ayurveda, chronic stress management, and non-pharmaceutical therapeutic approaches continues to grow internationally, authentic Ayurveda, delivered by institutions with the clinical depth and track record that IVAC represents , is well placed to serve a market that is both large and increasingly discerning.About Indus Valley Ayurvedic Centre (IVAC)Indus Valley Ayurvedic Centre is a physician-founded, physician-supervised Ayurvedic healing institution based in Mysuru, Karnataka, India. Established over 25 years ago by Dr. Talavane Krishna, IVAC specialises in classical Panchakarma therapy, personalised Ayurvedic treatment programmes, and preventive healthcare through authentic Vedic protocols. The centre has hosted more than 50,000 guests from over 65 countries and holds a 4.8-star guest satisfaction rating.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: Your AI strategy is stalling in a place you're not looking: The data backlog
description: Most boards now have an AI strategy. Far fewer have noticed where it actually breaks.It rarely breaks at the model. It breaks several layers down, in the unglamorous work of getting clean, governed, current data to the place the model needs it. That work is still done largely by hand, one pipeline at a time, by a data team that was already at capacity before the AI mandate arrived. The strategy is sound. The supply chain feeding it isn't.For a Chief Data and Analytics Officer, this is the defining tension of 2026. Demand for data products is rising exponentially. The team building them grows, at best, in a straight line. No amount of executive urgency closes that gap, because it isn't a motivation problem. It's an architecture problem, and it deserves to be treated as one at the board level.The hidden tax on every initiativeConsider where a typical data team's hours actually go. Industry-wide, the majority of effort, often cited at 60 to 70%, goes into maintaining and fixing pipelines that already exist, not building the new ones the business is asking for. Every fire drill, every schema change that breaks a downstream report, every undocumented transformation someone has to reverse-engineer, is capacity quietly drained away from strategic work.This is the silent tax of the AI economy. It doesn't appear as a line item. It appears as a backlog that never shrinks, AI initiatives that slip another quarter, and a data leader increasingly cast as the gatekeeper of scarcity rather than the architect of advantage.Traditional automation was supposed to fix this, and it helped at the margins. But the integration platforms and workflow schedulers of the last decade automate tasks, not thinking. You still needed an engineer to design every pipeline, troubleshoot every failure, and rebuild every transformation when requirements changed. The work got faster. It didn't get smarter, and it didn't get smaller.A different equationThe shift now under way changes the equation rather than tuning it. The emerging category, AI Data Automation, moves the operational burden of data work, the building, testing, documenting, and maintaining, to autonomous AI agents, while keeping humans in command of strategy and governance. Maia, the platform built by data technology company Matillion, is one example of this approach in production.The distinction that matters for a CDAO is this: earlier tools made your existing team faster. This approach decouples output from headcount entirely. The volume of data work your organisation can deliver stops being a function of how many engineers you can hire and retain. For any leader who has tried to scale a specialist team in a competitive talent market, that is a structurally different proposition.The numbers from early adopters make the point concretely. A two-person data team at Edmund Optics, a global optics manufacturer with 34,000 product lines, used this model to revive a critical pipeline that had been stalled for a year and had already burned $50,000 in failed attempts. They rebuilt it in an afternoon, tripled their output, and saved $100,000 in consulting spend, with no new hires. At St. James's Place, a UK wealth management firm, an analysis process that consumed 4,000 hours a year was reduced to roughly 16 hours. Crucially, that happened inside a regulated financial services environment, with governance and audit standards intact.The governance question, answered properlyThat last point is where many executives, rightly, grow cautious. Autonomy is worthless to a serious enterprise if it comes at the cost of control. An AI system that builds pipelines quickly but ignores your compliance obligations or architecture standards is not an efficiency gain. It's a risk you've automated.The credible systems in this category are built to address exactly that. They operate within a governed foundation where every action is logged and every transformation is traceable. They capture business rules and governance requirements before executing, rather than guessing at them. This is what separates a genuine enterprise capability from an impressive demonstration, and it's the question a CDAO should press hardest in any evaluation. The right answer isn't "trust the AI." It's "here is the audit trail."What changes for the leaderHandled well, this does more than clear a backlog. It changes the strategic position of the data function itself.A CDAO spends much of their time today rationing. Which initiative gets resourced. Which request waits. Which modernisation effort is deferred so the lights stay on. The role becomes one of saying no, carefully and often. When the operational layer of data work is automated, that calculus shifts. Modernisation and new AI innovation can run in parallel rather than competing for the same scarce engineers. The default answer to a strategic request can move from "not this quarter" to "yes."The team changes with it. Engineers move off the treadmill of pipeline maintenance and onto data product ownership and architecture, the work that actually compounds in value. That's a more defensible team to build and a more attractive one to join, which matters in a market where data talent is hard to win and harder to keep.The board-level framingFor enterprises scaling AI ambitions against real talent constraints, the framing is worth getting right. The question is not "which data tool should we buy?" It's "what is the operating model for data work that lets our AI strategy actually execute?"Buying another tool that makes a stretched team marginally faster doesn't answer that question. Changing the model does. The organisations that will define the next decade aren't simply adopting AI features. They're rethinking, deliberately and at the top, how data work gets done at all.A detailed guide for data leaders on this shift is set out in this guide for CDAOs.Produced in partnership with Matillion, the data technology company behind the Maia AI data platform.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

description: Sources suggest Musk may be mulling big donation to Trump Accounts.
content:
One way Donald Trump plans to celebrate July 4th is by rolling out children’s savings accounts, known as Trump Accounts, and apparently, he’s been angling to get a big donation from SpaceX as part of that push.
A person familiar with talks involving the Trump administration told Semafor that SpaceX has spoken with US officials about donating stock to Trump Accounts. At this stage, however, it remains unclear if a donation will be made or what impact a SpaceX donation might have on the value of the accounts, which Trump hopes will be a defining part of his legacy.
So far, 6 million children have been signed up for Trump Accounts, which officially launch next week on Independence Day. Trump accounts are a new type of individual retirement account available to any child under 18 with a Social Security number, and parents can sign up their kids through a Trump Accounts app that's already available for download. As an extra perk, any child born between 2025 and 2028 can receive a one-time $1,000 contribution from the government.Read full article
